heroOverview: Torticollis (ICD-10: M43.6) is a cervical muscle disorder in which involuntary or fixed contraction of one or more neck muscles causes the head to tilt to one side and rotate toward the opposite shoulder, producing the distinctive twisted-neck posture that gives the condition its Latin name — from tortus (twisted) and collum (neck). Acquired acute torticollis is common, affecting an estimated 10–12% of adults at least once in their lives as a transient episode. Cervical dystonia — the chronic neurological form — affects roughly 60,000 people in the United States and is the most common focal dystonia. Congenital muscular torticollis is present at birth or develops in infancy due to sternocleidomastoid muscle fibrosis and is one of the most frequent musculoskeletal abnormalities seen in neonates, occurring in approximately 3–16 per 1,000 live births. Treatment ranges from physiotherapy and stretching in congenital and acute cases to botulinum toxin injections, which resolve symptoms in more than 70% of patients with cervical dystonia.

keyStats.prevalence: Cervical dystonia affects approximately 60,000 people in the U.S.; congenital muscular torticollis occurs in 3–16 per 1,000 live births
keyStats.demographics: Congenital form: neonates and infants; acute acquired form: adults of any age; cervical dystonia: peak onset ages 30–60, women affected 1.5–2 times more than men
keyStats.avgAge: Cervical dystonia onset typically in the fourth to sixth decade; congenital form presents at birth or within the first few weeks of life
keyStats.globalCases: Focal dystonias including cervical dystonia affect an estimated 500,000 people in North America and Europe combined

definition: Torticollis (ICD-10: M43.6) is a postural disorder of the cervical spine defined by involuntary or persistent lateral tilt of the head, usually combined with rotation of the chin toward the contralateral shoulder, caused by abnormal contraction or shortening of cervical muscles, most often the sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius. The condition is classified by aetiology into three broad categories. Congenital muscular torticollis arises from fibrosis within the sternocleidomastoid, often attributed to birth trauma, in utero positioning, or ischaemia; it accounts for the majority of cases in infants and typically presents as a palpable fibrous mass or tight band in the muscle. Acquired acute (non-muscular) torticollis is the most common form in adults and results from muscle spasm triggered by minor trauma, awkward sleep posture, cervical spine subluxation, or upper respiratory infection; it resolves within days to weeks in most patients. Cervical dystonia (spasmodic torticollis) is a chronic neurological condition in which basal ganglia circuit dysfunction drives sustained involuntary contraction of cervical muscles; it is the most disabling form and often persists for life without treatment. Additional causes include atlanto-axial rotatory subluxation, Grisel syndrome (post-pharyngeal inflammatory subluxation in children), posterior fossa tumours, Sandifer syndrome, and cervical spondylosis. Specialist involvement includes orthopaedics, neurology for dystonia, and physiotherapy.

typesClassification.description: Present from birth or early infancy, caused by sternocleidomastoid muscle fibrosis. The head tilts toward the affected muscle and rotates away. Most cases respond to physiotherapy stretching if started before six months of age.

A sudden-onset, painful cervical muscle spasm in adults, typically waking after sleep in an abnormal position. The neck locks in a tilted and rotated posture. Most episodes resolve spontaneously within days to one week.

A chronic focal dystonia with sustained or intermittent involuntary muscle contractions of the neck; the most common focal dystonia in adults, requiring long-term management with botulinum toxin or medication.

A structural subluxation of the C1-C2 joint causing a fixed head tilt and rotation, seen after trauma or as Grisel syndrome following head-and-neck infection in children; requires urgent orthopaedic or neurosurgical assessment.
Head tilt caused by an identifiable underlying cause such as a posterior fossa tumour, vestibular disorder, ocular muscle palsy, or cervical disc herniation; the primary condition must be treated.

primarySymptoms: Involuntary or fixed lateral head tilt, with the ear drawn toward one shoulder, that does not correct fully with voluntary effort.
Rotation of the chin toward the opposite shoulder from the direction of tilt, creating the characteristic twisted-neck posture.
Neck pain and muscle aching on the side of the contracting muscles, ranging from mild discomfort to severe pain that limits all movement.
Palpable hardness or a fibrous cord within the sternocleidomastoid muscle on the side of the tilt.
Restricted range of cervical motion — particularly difficulty rotating the head to look over the affected shoulder.
Intermittent or sustained involuntary jerking or tremor of the head in cervical dystonia.
A sensory trick (geste antagoniste) — many patients with cervical dystonia discover that lightly touching the chin, cheek, or back of the head temporarily reduces the abnormal posture.
Headache at the base of the skull or across the upper trapezius, most prominent after prolonged upright activity.
Facial asymmetry or plagiocephaly (skull flattening) in infants with untreated congenital muscular torticollis.
Shoulder elevation on the affected side as the neck muscles chronically shorten and pull the shoulder upward.
Sleep disturbance due to pain when unable to find a comfortable head position at night.
earlyWarningSigns: Waking with a stiff, painful neck that resists rotation to one side after sleeping in an unusual position.
A parent noticing an infant consistently turning the head to one side and resisting turning the other way.
A palpable, firm mass or tight band noticed in an infant's neck within the first few weeks of birth.
Increasing frequency of involuntary head movements or neck muscle contractions in an adult over several weeks.
emergencySigns: Torticollis with fever, sore throat, and difficulty swallowing in a child, suggesting Grisel syndrome or retropharyngeal abscess requiring urgent imaging.

Sudden onset torticollis after neck trauma, particularly with any neurological symptoms (arm weakness, numbness, pins-and-needles), indicating possible cervical spine instability — do not manipulate the neck.
New torticollis with headache, vomiting, or visual changes, raising concern for a posterior fossa lesion or raised intracranial pressure.
Torticollis with progressive arm or leg weakness, suggesting cervical myelopathy needing urgent neurosurgical assessment.

causes.description: Fibrosis within the sternocleidomastoid during fetal development or birth — possibly from a compartment-syndrome-like ischaemia during a difficult delivery or prolonged in-utero positioning — produces a palpable mass and muscle shortening that pulls the head into a fixed tilt.

causes.cause: Cervical muscle spasm from minor trauma or posture
causes.description: Awkward sleep position, a sudden turning movement, prolonged forward-head screen posture, or a minor whiplash event can trigger protective cervical muscle spasm in the sternocleidomastoid or paraspinal muscles, producing an acute painful tilt that usually resolves within days.

In cervical dystonia, abnormal neuronal signalling in the cortico-basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical loop drives involuntary, sustained muscle contraction; the exact initiating event is unknown but genetic variants in TOR1A (DYT1) and other loci, plus environmental triggers, are implicated in a subset of cases.

Rotatory displacement of C1 on C2 — from trauma, ligamentous laxity in Down syndrome, or post-infectious inflammation in children (Grisel syndrome) — physically locks the head in a tilted, rotated position and accounts for cases that do not resolve with conservative measures.

causes.cause: Upper respiratory infection and local inflammation
causes.description: Pharyngeal inflammation, retropharyngeal abscess, or lymphadenitis can spread to the transverse ligament of the atlas via pharyngovertebral venous channels, causing Grisel syndrome with painfully locked head rotation in children.

Certain medications — particularly first-generation antipsychotics, metoclopramide, and prochlorperazine — block dopamine receptors and cause acute cervical dystonic reactions within hours of ingestion, producing torticollis that resolves rapidly with anticholinergic antidote such as benztropine.

riskFactors.description: Forceps or vacuum extraction delivery increases the risk of sternocleidomastoid trauma and subsequent fibrosis; birth position factors also play a role in congenital muscular torticollis.
Constrained in-utero positioning in primiparous pregnancies or large infants increases risk of sternocleidomastoid ischaemia and congenital torticollis.

Cervical dystonia has a genetic component; approximately 10–25% of patients have a first-degree relative with focal dystonia, and pathogenic variants in several dystonia-related genes have been identified.
Cervical dystonia onset is preceded by a peripheral neck injury in approximately 15–20% of cases; even seemingly minor injuries can apparently trigger the condition in susceptible individuals.
Women develop cervical dystonia 1.5–2 times more often than men; the biological basis is unclear but may relate to hormonal influences on basal ganglia circuitry.

Use of antipsychotics, antiemetics, and other dopamine antagonists raises the risk of acute dystonic reactions including torticollis; dose reduction or switching to an atypical agent reduces this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged forward-head posture and screen use
riskFactors.description: Sustained awkward neck postures in office work or screen use predispose to acute muscle spasm and recurrent wry-neck episodes.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants, where congenital muscular torticollis is one of the most common musculoskeletal diagnoses
School-age children and adolescents, particularly for Grisel syndrome following upper respiratory illness
Adults aged 20–50 for acute acquired wry-neck episodes
Women aged 30–60 are the predominant group for cervical dystonia
People taking dopamine-blocking medications at any age for drug-induced acute dystonic reactions
Athletes and manual workers whose occupation involves repetitive or extreme neck positions
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a careful history of onset, duration, pain character, any preceding illness or injury, medication use, and family history. Acute wry neck in an adult after poor sleep posture is diagnosed clinically without imaging. In infants, the clinician palpates the sternocleidomastoid for a fibrous mass, measures passive cervical rotation and lateral flexion, and checks for facial asymmetry and plagiocephaly. Cervical dystonia is a clinical neurological diagnosis based on the characteristic pattern of involuntary sustained or jerky head movements, the presence of sensory tricks, and exclusion of structural causes. Plain radiography of the cervical spine is ordered when trauma, atlanto-axial subluxation, or bony anomaly is suspected. CT is used urgently when spinal instability or a retropharyngeal collection is possible. MRI of the cervical spine and posterior fossa is indicated for atypical cases, progressive neurological signs, or suspected secondary torticollis from a tumour or syrinx. Electromyography helps characterise the pattern of muscle involvement in cervical dystonia and guides botulinum toxin injection targeting. Ophthalmological assessment is done to exclude ocular torticollis when there is no cervical muscle cause. In children with persistent torticollis, ultrasound of the neck identifies sternocleidomastoid fibrosis and is the initial imaging of choice to avoid radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measure the degree of head tilt, rotation, and flexion restriction to classify severity and monitor treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks you to move your head in different directions and measures the angles; painless and takes about 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In infants, identify the fibrous mass or thickening within the sternocleidomastoid and confirm congenital muscular torticollis diagnosis without radiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe is applied to the neck with gel; quick, painless, and well tolerated even by newborns.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect atlanto-axial subluxation, cervical vertebral anomalies, or bony instability underlying the torticollis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard AP and lateral X-rays taken standing or lying; takes a few minutes with minimal radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the cervical spine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid, detailed bony imaging when atlanto-axial subluxation, fracture, or retropharyngeal abscess needs urgent exclusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a scanner for 3–5 minutes; no preparation needed; involves ionising radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the cervical spine and brain
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluate soft tissue, nerve root compression, posterior fossa tumour, or other secondary causes in atypical or progressive torticollis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in the scanner for 30–45 minutes; no radiation; may include a contrast injection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify which muscles are overactive in cervical dystonia to guide precise botulinum toxin injection targeting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fine needle electrodes are inserted into neck muscles while you hold various positions; mild discomfort; takes 30–60 minutes with a specialist neurologist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is tailored to the type of torticollis. Congenital muscular torticollis in infants is treated with physiotherapy stretching — manual passive stretching of the sternocleidomastoid by a trained physiotherapist or taught parent technique — started as early as possible; more than 90% of cases resolve with conservative treatment if begun before one year of age. Surgery (sternocleidomastoid release) is reserved for the 10–15% who fail physiotherapy, especially when diagnosed late. Acute acquired wry neck in adults is managed conservatively with NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, heat, and gentle mobilisation; most episodes resolve within three to seven days, and physiotherapy or manipulation by an osteopath or physiotherapist can accelerate recovery. Cervical dystonia, the most challenging form, is a chronic condition requiring long-term treatment. Botulinum toxin injections into the overactive cervical muscles — the current gold-standard therapy — reduce symptoms in more than 70% of patients and must be repeated every 10–12 weeks. Oral medications including anticholinergic drugs (trihexyphenidyl), baclofen, clonazepam, and tetrabenazine provide modest adjunctive benefit. Deep brain stimulation of the globus pallidus internus is reserved for severe, medically refractory cervical dystonia. Acute drug-induced dystonic reactions are reversed rapidly with intravenous or intramuscular benztropine or diphenhydramine. Physiotherapy, postural training, and biofeedback are useful adjuncts across all forms.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injections (Botox / Dysport)
medicalTreatments.description: Chemodenervation of the overactive cervical muscles by injection of botulinum toxin A is the first-line treatment for cervical dystonia. The toxin blocks acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction, temporarily relaxing the abnormally contracting muscles. Injections are repeated every 10–12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meaningful symptom reduction in 70–90% of patients; approximately 10–20% develop neutralising antibodies over time reducing efficacy.

medicalTreatments.name: Trihexyphenidyl (anticholinergic)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral anticholinergic agent that reduces involuntary muscle activity through central muscarinic blockade; used as adjunctive therapy in cervical dystonia, particularly in younger patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in approximately 40–50% of patients at therapeutic doses; limited by dose-dependent anticholinergic side effects including dry mouth, blurred vision, and cognitive effects.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and muscle relaxants (acute torticollis)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral NSAIDs such as naproxen or ibuprofen combined with a short course of muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine, diazepam) relieve cervical spasm and pain in acute wry neck.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective pain and spasm relief in approximately 80–90% of acute cases within 3–7 days; no role in chronic cervical dystonia.

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen or clonazepam (adjunctive)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-B agonist (baclofen) and benzodiazepine (clonazepam) reduce central motor excitability in cervical dystonia and are used when botulinum toxin alone provides insufficient control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest additional symptom reduction in 30–50% of patients with cervical dystonia when added to botulinum toxin.

medicalTreatments.name: Benztropine (acute drug-induced dystonia)
medicalTreatments.description: Parenteral anticholinergic drug administered intramuscularly or intravenously for acute dystonic reactions caused by dopamine-blocking medications, including acute torticollis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid reversal of drug-induced dystonic reaction in more than 90% of cases within 10–20 minutes of administration.

surgicalOptions.name: Sternocleidomastoid muscle release (tenotomy or myotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical division of the tight sternocleidomastoid and its fibrous band in congenital muscular torticollis that has failed physiotherapy or was diagnosed after age one; the muscle is released at one or both ends.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good to excellent head posture correction in approximately 85–95% of cases when performed before age 10; outcomes decline with later surgery due to compensatory skeletal changes.

surgicalOptions.name: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the globus pallidus internus
surgicalOptions.description: Implanted electrodes deliver continuous electrical stimulation to the GPi to normalise motor circuit activity in severe, medically refractory cervical dystonia; response develops gradually over 3–12 months post-implantation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mean improvement of approximately 50–70% on the Toronto Western Spasmodic Torticollis Rating Scale in carefully selected patients; effects are sustained at 3–5 year follow-up.

surgicalOptions.name: Selective peripheral denervation (Bertrand procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical denervation of the cervical muscles contributing to the abnormal posture in cervical dystonia; an alternative to DBS for patients who fail botulinum toxin and do not wish implantable hardware.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in approximately 60–75% of selected patients; the procedure is highly technique-dependent and performed at specialist centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and stretching programme
alternativeTreatments.description: Manual stretching, proprioceptive exercises, and postural retraining are the mainstay of congenital muscular torticollis and a useful adjunct in all forms; structured programmes supervised by a paediatric or cervical physiotherapist achieve resolution in more than 90% of infants treated early.

alternativeTreatments.name: Osteopathic or chiropractic mobilisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Gentle cervical mobilisation techniques can accelerate resolution of acute wry neck in adults; high-velocity manipulation should be avoided until imaging has excluded structural instability.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sensory biofeedback training
alternativeTreatments.description: Electromyographic biofeedback teaches patients with cervical dystonia to consciously reduce muscle activity using real-time visual or auditory feedback; benefit is modest and best used alongside botulinum toxin.

whySeeSpecialist: Any child whose head tilt persists beyond two weeks or does not respond to home stretching needs a physiotherapist or paediatric orthopaedic assessment before three months of age, because the window for non-surgical cure closes quickly. Adults with torticollis that does not resolve in seven to ten days, any torticollis after significant neck trauma, or involuntary neck movements that recur and worsen should see a neurologist or orthopaedic spine specialist. Botulinum toxin injection for cervical dystonia must be performed by a neurologist or movement disorder specialist trained in EMG-guided neck injection.
doctorSelectionGuide: For congenital muscular torticollis in infants, seek a paediatric physiotherapist with experience in cervical stretching and, if surgery is needed, a paediatric orthopaedic surgeon. For cervical dystonia, a movement disorder neurologist at a centre that performs EMG-guided botulinum toxin injections gives the best outcomes. For acute or recurrent adult wry neck, a physiotherapist specialising in the cervical spine is usually sufficient. Ask whether the botulinum toxin injector uses EMG guidance, which improves injection accuracy and treatment response.
preventionStrategies: Maintain neutral cervical posture during prolonged screen or desk work; position screens at eye level and take movement breaks every 30–60 minutes.
Sleep on a supportive pillow that keeps the neck in neutral alignment and avoid sleeping prone, which rotates and extends the neck for hours.
Warm up cervical muscles before physical activity and cool down afterwards, particularly before contact sports or activities with sudden head movements.
Ensure infants receive a variety of head positions during awake time (tummy time, alternating sides when feeding) to prevent preferential sternocleidomastoid shortening.
Report new-onset head tilt in an infant to a healthcare provider promptly for early physiotherapy referral.
If taking dopamine-blocking medications, report any neck stiffness or involuntary head movement immediately so the drug can be adjusted.
lifestyleModifications: Use ergonomic workstation set-up with monitor at eye level and keyboard at a height preventing neck flexion.
Apply warmth to the neck before physiotherapy stretching exercises — a warm shower or heat pack for 10 minutes relaxes the muscles.
Learn and use the personal sensory trick (geste antagoniste) that temporarily reduces dystonic posture, providing relief throughout the day.
Reduce stress through consistent sleep, relaxation techniques, and exercise — stress reliably worsens cervical dystonia symptoms.
Avoid driving long distances without cervical rest breaks if rotation and tilt are triggered by sustained positions.
Use a neck collar only as directed by a clinician; prolonged collar use in adults leads to deconditioning.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids to support general muscle and joint health
Adequate magnesium intake from nuts, seeds, and leafy greens, as magnesium plays a role in normal muscle relaxation
Calcium and vitamin D for skeletal health in patients who will require prolonged physiotherapy
Good hydration to support muscle recovery and reduce cramp tendency
avoid: Excessive caffeine, which can increase muscle excitability and worsen involuntary contractions in cervical dystonia
Alcohol, which interacts with muscle relaxants and benzodiazepines used in treatment
Dehydration, which can trigger muscle cramps and spasms in the cervical muscles

exerciseGuidelines: For congenital muscular torticollis, regular daily passive stretching of the affected sternocleidomastoid (as instructed by a physiotherapist) is the primary treatment; active strengthening of the contralateral cervical muscles follows once passive range is restored. For cervical dystonia, swimming and gentle aqua-therapy are particularly beneficial, as buoyancy reduces muscle loading. Cervical-stabilising exercises focusing on deep neck flexor activation improve postural control. Avoid contact sports or exercises involving sudden, forceful neck rotation until cleared by a specialist. For acute wry neck, return to normal activity as symptoms allow, avoiding sudden movements that reproduce sharp pain.
dailyManagement: For infants with congenital muscular torticollis: perform prescribed stretching exercises three times daily, positioning the infant to encourage the head to turn toward the tight side during feeding and play.
For cervical dystonia: attend scheduled botulinum toxin injection appointments every 10–12 weeks; do not miss injections as symptoms typically return within two weeks of the injection wearing off.
Keep a symptom diary noting pain severity, triggering positions, and sensory trick effectiveness to inform injection dose adjustments at each clinic visit.
Apply heat or gentle massage to sore neck muscles after activities that provoke symptoms.
Notify your neurologist immediately if neck injections become less effective, as a change of botulinum toxin product, dose, or injection sites may restore response.
prognosis: Outcomes vary markedly by type. Congenital muscular torticollis carries an excellent prognosis when physiotherapy begins before six months of age — resolution rates exceed 90% without surgery. Cases treated between six and twelve months still do well, but success rates decline with age, and surgery is typically needed after age one year. Acute adult wry neck almost always resolves completely within one to two weeks with or without treatment. Cervical dystonia is a chronic condition; spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 10–20% of patients, particularly within the first year, but relapse is common. With regular botulinum toxin injections, the majority of patients achieve good functional control, maintaining the ability to work and engage in daily activities, though complete abolition of abnormal posture is uncommon. Quality of life is significantly impaired if treatment is inadequate, particularly because neck pain, sleep disturbance, and occupational disability are frequent.
recoveryTimeline: Acute wry neck resolves within three to seven days for most adults with analgesics and muscle relaxants. Congenital torticollis treated with physiotherapy typically shows measurable improvement within six to eight weeks and near-complete resolution within six months when started early. After sternocleidomastoid release surgery in children, postoperative physiotherapy begins within one to two weeks and functional improvement continues for up to one year. Cervical dystonia responds to botulinum toxin within seven to fourteen days of injection, with peak effect at four to six weeks; the injection cycle needs to be repeated indefinitely.
complications: Permanent facial asymmetry and skull plagiocephaly in infants with congenital torticollis diagnosed and treated late.
Cervical spondylosis and accelerated disc degeneration from chronic abnormal head posture and asymmetric loading in untreated or inadequately treated cervical dystonia.
Depression and social isolation, which affect approximately 25–30% of patients with cervical dystonia due to chronic pain and visible head movement.
Dysphagia and breathing difficulty in severe cervical dystonia where posterior neck muscles pull the head into retrocollis.
Botulinum toxin resistance due to neutralising antibody formation, occurring in approximately 5–10% of long-term users and managed by switching product formulation or dose adjustment.
Cervical myelopathy in patients with atlanto-axial subluxation not treated promptly, resulting in potentially irreversible spinal cord damage.

supportResources.name: Dystonia Europe — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient-focused resources on cervical dystonia, treatment options, and support networks across Europe.

supportResources.name: Dystonia Medical Research Foundation (DMRF)
supportResources.description: U.S.-based foundation providing information, research updates, and peer support for people living with cervical dystonia.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Dystonia Information Page
supportResources.description: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke overview of dystonia including cervical forms, treatment, and research.

sources.title: Jankovic J. Medical treatment of cervical dystonia. Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and Psychiatry, 2006
Bhidayasiri R. Differential diagnosis of common tremor syndromes and dystonia. Practical Neurology, 2005
Lee EH et al. Congenital muscular torticollis: long-term follow-up. Journal of Pediatric Orthopaedics, 1986
Patel K et al. Cervical dystonia: epidemiology, clinical features, and update on botulinum neurotoxin treatment. Tremor and Other Hyperkinetic Movements, 2014
Castillo-Bustamante M et al. Botulinum toxin for cervical dystonia: a systematic review. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2005

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervicalgia (neck pain)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervicalgia is generalised neck pain without a fixed abnormal head posture; torticollis is defined by the involuntary twisted head position, though pain is also present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervical radiculopathy causes neck pain with arm radiation and neurological deficits from nerve root compression; torticollis causes a postural head deviation without the arm symptoms unless an underlying disc herniation coexists.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankylosing spondylitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankylosing spondylitis causes progressive spinal stiffness and bilateral sacroiliac inflammation with elevated inflammatory markers; torticollis is asymmetric and postural, not an inflammatory arthropathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scoliosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scoliosis is a lateral curvature of the thoracic and lumbar spine; cervical torticollis is limited to the neck, though a high thoracic curve can sometimes produce apparent head tilt.

relatedConditions.name: Cervicalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Neck pain is almost always present in torticollis and may be the predominant symptom in acute or cervical dystonia forms.

relatedConditions.name: Cervical radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Disc herniation causing nerve root compression is a recognised secondary cause of torticollis and may coexist with cervical dystonia.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Advanced ankylosing spondylitis causes cervical stiffness and in late disease a fixed forward-flexed posture of the neck that can superficially resemble torticollis.

relatedConditions.name: Scoliosis
relatedConditions.relevance: High thoracic scoliosis can produce a compensatory head tilt; both conditions may coexist in children with congenital spinal anomalies.

faqs.question: What is torticollis?
faqs.answer: Torticollis, also called wry neck, is a condition where one or more neck muscles pull the head into a tilted and rotated position. It can be a sudden painful spasm lasting a few days, a structural abnormality present from birth, or a chronic neurological condition called cervical dystonia.

faqs.question: What causes torticollis in babies?
faqs.answer: Congenital muscular torticollis in babies is caused by fibrosis or tightening of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, usually from birth trauma, prolonged pressure on the muscle in utero, or reduced blood flow during delivery. A firm lump may be felt in the neck and the baby consistently tilts the head to one side.

faqs.question: Is torticollis painful?
faqs.answer: Acute wry neck in adults is usually quite painful, with the neck locked in a tilted position and sharp pain on attempting to straighten it. Cervical dystonia produces pain in approximately 70 to 80% of patients, ranging from a dull ache to severe muscle cramping. Congenital torticollis in infants is generally not painful.

faqs.question: Can torticollis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Acute wry neck in adults almost always resolves on its own within three to seven days with rest, heat, and pain relief. Congenital muscular torticollis in infants has an excellent chance of resolving with physiotherapy stretching if started early. Cervical dystonia is chronic and does not go away without treatment.

faqs.question: How is torticollis treated in adults?
faqs.answer: Acute wry neck is treated with NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, heat, and physiotherapy. Cervical dystonia is treated primarily with botulinum toxin injections into the overactive neck muscles every 10 to 12 weeks, which reduce symptoms in more than 70% of patients. Oral medications provide additional symptom control.

faqs.question: What is cervical dystonia?
faqs.answer: Cervical dystonia, also called spasmodic torticollis, is a chronic neurological condition where abnormal brain signals cause sustained involuntary contraction of cervical muscles, pulling the head into a twisted position. It is the most common focal dystonia and requires long-term management with botulinum toxin injections.

faqs.question: How is torticollis treated in babies?
faqs.answer: The main treatment for congenital muscular torticollis in babies is daily physiotherapy stretching of the tight sternocleidomastoid muscle, which parents are taught to perform at home. More than 90% of infants treated before one year of age recover fully. Surgery is needed in the minority whose torticollis does not resolve with physiotherapy.

faqs.question: What does botulinum toxin do for torticollis?
faqs.answer: Botulinum toxin is injected into the neck muscles that are contracting abnormally in cervical dystonia. It blocks the nerve signals that cause the muscles to contract, relaxing them and allowing the head to return toward a normal position. The effect lasts 10 to 12 weeks and injections need to be repeated.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for torticollis?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if your neck has been locked in a tilted position for more than a week, if the torticollis follows a significant neck injury, or if you have arm numbness, weakness, headache, or fever alongside the head tilt. Any child whose head consistently tilts to one side should be seen by a doctor promptly.

faqs.question: Can torticollis cause permanent damage?
faqs.answer: Untreated congenital muscular torticollis in children can cause permanent facial asymmetry and skull flattening. Cervical dystonia, if poorly controlled, causes progressive cervical spine degeneration. Atlanto-axial subluxation left untreated can result in spinal cord compression. Early treatment greatly reduces these risks.

faqs.question: Is torticollis a form of dystonia?
faqs.answer: Cervical dystonia is a form of focal dystonia that produces torticollis. Not all torticollis is dystonia — acute wry neck from muscle spasm and congenital muscular torticollis have different mechanisms. When involuntary, sustained, or repetitive neck muscle contractions are the cause, the condition is classified as dystonia.

faqs.question: What is the sensory trick in cervical dystonia?
faqs.answer: Many people with cervical dystonia find that lightly touching a specific point on the chin, cheek, or back of the head temporarily reduces the abnormal head posture. This is called a sensory trick or geste antagoniste. It works by temporarily overriding the abnormal motor signals and is a useful coping strategy throughout the day.

faqs.question: Can wry neck be caused by sleeping badly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sleeping in an awkward position — particularly with the head unsupported or twisted overnight — is one of the most common triggers for acute wry neck in adults. The neck muscles go into protective spasm and the head becomes locked in a tilted position, usually resolving within a few days.

faqs.question: Can medications cause torticollis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dopamine-blocking medications including antipsychotics and anti-nausea drugs such as metoclopramide can cause acute dystonic reactions within hours of a dose, producing sudden torticollis. This reverses quickly with an anticholinergic drug such as benztropine. Anyone who develops neck twisting after starting a new medication should seek urgent medical attention.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for cervical dystonia?
faqs.answer: There is no curative treatment that permanently eliminates cervical dystonia. Botulinum toxin injections provide good symptom control for most patients but need to be repeated indefinitely. Deep brain stimulation is an option for severe refractory cases. Spontaneous remission occurs in about 10 to 20% of patients within the first year.

faqs.question: Does torticollis affect vision?
faqs.answer: Persistent head tilt from any cause can strain the eye muscles over time as the visual system compensates to keep gaze level. Untreated congenital torticollis in children can lead to amblyopia (lazy eye) from prolonged abnormal head position. An ophthalmology review is part of the assessment for persistent childhood torticollis.

faqs.question: How long do botulinum toxin injections last for cervical dystonia?
faqs.answer: The effect of a botulinum toxin injection for cervical dystonia typically begins within 7 to 14 days, reaches maximum benefit at 4 to 6 weeks, and wears off over 10 to 12 weeks. Injections are therefore repeated every 3 months on an ongoing basis.

faqs.question: Can physiotherapy treat cervical dystonia?
faqs.answer: Physiotherapy alone does not treat cervical dystonia but is a valuable adjunct to botulinum toxin. Cervical stabilising exercises, proprioceptive training, and postural retraining improve function and reduce pain. Physiotherapy combined with botulinum toxin consistently outperforms either alone in clinical studies.

faqs.question: What is Grisel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Grisel syndrome is atlanto-axial subluxation — a partial dislocation of the first and second cervical vertebrae — caused by inflammation spreading from a throat infection or head-and-neck surgery to the ligaments of the upper cervical spine in children. It produces a painful fixed head tilt and requires urgent imaging and orthopaedic review.

faqs.question: Can torticollis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Acute wry neck can recur with another episode of bad posture or minor neck trauma. Congenital muscular torticollis can recur if stretching is stopped too early during infancy. Cervical dystonia symptoms return within weeks of a botulinum toxin injection wearing off, which is why the injection cycle is maintained continuously.

metaDescription: Torticollis causes the head to tilt and rotate due to neck muscle contraction. Learn about wry neck, cervical dystonia, botulinum toxin and physiotherapy.
